posts.cooked:
So: a question for sauternes experts. Am really starting to get into this wine.

I know people have their favorite house style or best QPR, or greatest bottle, but but I’m looking for something different.

If, this coming year, I wanted to taste benchmarks from Sauternes or Barsac for:

the most botrytis-displaying
the greatest acidity
the greatest length
the most overstuffed,
the greatest delicacy
the greatest complexity

what producer & vintage should I get for each of the above characteristics (separately!).

Obviously, as you go down this list the judgement becomes more subjective.

Also I’m not looking for ultra-flawed wine, but if the bottle is somewhat unbalanced, that’s OK!

Also, reccs should exclude d’Yquem which is beyond my budget alas.

(this q was inspired by AAgrawal’s post here
TN: All-Sauternes Dinner (Yquem, Climens, Coutet, Fargues, Doisy Daene))

link_counts.title: TN: All-Sauternes Dinner (Yquem, Climens, Coutet, Fargues, Suduiraut, Doisy Daene)
TN: All-Sauternes Dinner (Yquem, Climens, Coutet, Fargues, Suduiraut, Doisy Daene)

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
These are all subjective:
the most botrytis-displaying → Maybe more vintage specific than producer specific. Maybe 2003, but keep in mind it was also warm.
the greatest acidity → Climens
the greatest length → I would say Fargues
the most overstuffed → Rieussec when young. But also very oaky when young
the greatest delicacy → Maybe Coutet
the greatest complexity → Variable, but another vote for Fargues here.

A lot of these are both producer specific and vintage specific, so it’s not a straight comparison. All of these above are some of my favorite producers.

Awesome reply thanks! Want to pick your (and other fora members brains more)

Is there an 03’ that just drips botrytis?

Are there particular vintages of

Climens for acidity,
Fargues for length or complexity
Rieussec for Overstuffed-ness

that come to mind?

I will go to CT and start looking at notes with this set of guidelines tho! Many thanks again!

posts.name: Dave Dewhurst
posts.cooked:
Doisy-Daene is one of my favourites in good vintages for depth, length and acidity. Have also enjoyed D’Arche across a number of vintages as well as Coutet.

Probably the most overstuffed I have had would be La Tour Blanche 2003, which came in at ~400g/l residual sugar if I remember rightly. Twas like drinking liquid barley sugars.

Cheers

Dave

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Dave_Dewhurst:

Doisy-Daene is one of my favourites in good vintages for depth, length and acidity. Have also enjoyed D’Arche across a number of vintages as well as Coutet.

I’ve never thought d’Arche was something interesting. For me, it has been a “nice wine” but always seemed like an underachiever among many Cru Classés.

Dave_Dewhurst:

Probably the most overstuffed I have had would be La Tour Blanche 2003, which came in at ~400g/l residual sugar if I remember rightly. Twas like drinking liquid barley sugars.

Was that number a hyperbole? If it wasn’t, FWIW, I think that amount is technically impossible for any Sauternes, ever. Basically the only styles of wine that can attain that level of residual sugar are Tokaji Essencia and PX. At that RS, making Sauternes would be impossible, since no yeast is able to ferment anything but a tiny bit of glucose (Essencia is typically fermented to 2-3% ABV over many years in oak barrels; PX is fortified to 17-20%). To qualify for Sauternes, the wine must be fermented to a minimum of 13% ABV, which would be impossible at ~400 g/l.

Anyways, it was pretty easy to check the RS for 2003 - it was 178 g/l. I can imagine it would feel pretty cloying, because that number is atypically high for a Sauternes, but that’s still a far cry from drinking a 400 g/l RS wine. Those are a completely different game altogether.

tour-blanche.com

LTB2003_uk.pdf

587.60 KB

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
1967, 1986, 1990 and 2001 Climens WINE TALK
At the end of our monthly group dinner last night, we had these four fabulous bottles of Climens. The 1990 was good, really good, but IMHO it did not quite hold up to the other three wines. The 1967 and 1986 were simply glorious. The 1967 looked like it should have been a bit over the hill. and when you first put it in you mouth you wonder if it has any fruit yet, but then while it is in your mouth (and after) the flavors start to explode and there are a lot of just marvelous older Climens fl…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The perceived sweetness doesn’t really track the sugar level in such a linear fashion (talking in general, not about LTB 2003 in particular, I should underline).

Sauternes/Barsac is all about the interplay between bitters (the extract that occurs when botrytis digests the grapes on the vine: the must looks like a purée, extraction from the skins is so high - so in a sense these are the ultimate orange wines) and the sweetness. If you lack botrytis you don’t have much in the way of bitters, so even with less sugar the wine will feel more cloying. Conversely, some of the analytically sweetest wines can be the least cloying/saacharine.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

The perceived sweetness doesn’t really track the sugar level in such a linear fashion (talking in general, not about LTB 2003 in particular, I should underline).

Entirely true. And it’s not just about the extracted bitter elements, but also things like alcohol, acidity, any other elements that come from botrytis (for example an ice wine and a botrytized wine that have similar levels of alcohol and acidity can be strikingly different), etc.

For example a PX at 400 g/l is something completely unlike an Essencia at 400 g/l!

However, a Sauternes is still a Sauternes. A Sauternes at 180 g/l RS must feel pretty lush and even cloying if the normal level one is used to is a Sauternes at 90-110 g/l - no matter how much there are balancing bitter elements. Even if I have not tasted that '03 LTB, I know this much having tasted some Sauternes wines that have clocked in at 150 g/l RS and above. I’m not saying they are unbalanced, buy they have been invariably noticeably sweeter with more richness compared to more typical Sauternes wines that have been hovering at around 100 g/l.

But no matter what kind the wine is, there is no way one could confuse it with a wine at 400 g/l - not because of just the sweetness but because these wines are just so very different from any kind of Sauternes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Trypical RS in the best vintages of Yquem is 145, with the 1945 the highest they have measured at 164g. So, 180 is definitely very high but equally 90-110 is too low for a great Sauternes picked in tris with good botrytis IMO.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Trypical RS in the best vintages of Yquem is 145, with the 1945 the highest they have measured at 164g. So, 180 is definitely very high but equally 90-110 is too low for a great Sauternes picked in tris with good botrytis IMO.

Correct, 90 g/l is maybe a bit too low for a typical Sauternes, but nothing outside the ordinary - checking out some wines I’ve tasted, I’ve had several. Probably not wines that’d classify as “great Sauternes”, though.

But further checking out some data sheets I could find, the typical levels seem to hover around 115-130 g/l; around 110 g/l and below for vintages with less botrytis; 140 g/l and above for hotter vintages and/or those with more botrytis.

posts.name: Dave Dewhurst
posts.cooked: Thanks Otto. Definitely a faulty memory on that one

OK, back on track

Doisy-Daene gives Coutet a run for the money in terms of delicacy. For me, only one sample, but the most overstuffed, funny enough was the Doisy-Daene L’Extravagant (though to be fair, I believe it was also a 2003). I can get onboard with Fargues for the greatest length, though some Climens and some Suduiraut can have very long finishes as well. Climens—sure, I can join the chorus there for most acidity, though it’s not like it’s a blazing level of difference.

Otherwise, all the comments Ashish makes are extremely a point. Good questions asked, Phil, thanks.

posts.name: Prashant Jyothi
posts.cooked: kind of off topic but I have a full bottle of 1990 Guiraud. It’s fairly dark. What can I expect out if it if assuming it was stored well

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Prashant_Jyothi:

kind of off topic but I have a full bottle of 1990 Guiraud. It’s fairly dark. What can I expect out if it if assuming it was stored well

In all likelihood it’s a great bottle of wine.

Many (but definitely not all) botrytized wines can turn remarkably dark in a relatively short time. I’ve seen Tokajis, Beerenausleses and Sauternes wines that have been dark as treacle, yet turned out to be surprisingly youthful in the nose and on the palate.

But then again, even with good provenance a wine +30 years old can be already past its peak. When you get to old wines, you don’t have great wines, only great bottles.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

No. Lots of Sauvignon Blanc in it, which shows aromatically, along with some passerillage character.

If you want to cellar a 2019 Sauternes I’d go with de Fargues.

I cannot think of any d’Yquem from a good, let alone great vintage that is not still showing well at 50 or more years. Yes, I know it’s 45% Sauvignon, (The highest ever) which gives it more freshness, and less opulence, making it a racy style, but I’m positive it’s a long term ager.

As much as I like de Fargues, I’ve never had any that were better at 40/50 than they were at 20. It doesn’t have the same aging potential as Yquem, but no Sauternes does.
